professor.name: Dr. Okafor
professor.text: We've been discussing how schools support student well-being this semester. Several districts have recently added a short daily mindfulness session to the school day, setting aside about ten minutes for breathing exercises and quiet reflection. Some educators argue this builds emotional regulation and lowers anxiety, while others believe it wastes instructional time that could go toward core subjects. Do you think schools should require a daily mindfulness period? Why or why not?

students.name: David
students.text: I think a required mindfulness period is worth the time. Adolescents face constant academic and social pressure, and a brief, structured pause gives them a concrete strategy for calming down. If students learn to regulate stress early, they tend to concentrate better afterward and retain more of the lesson.

students.name: Mia
students.text: I see why supervised reflection appeals to people, but making it mandatory feels counterproductive. Mindfulness works best when it is genuinely voluntary, and forcing restless teenagers to sit silently can breed resentment. Schools could instead offer it as an optional club so interested students benefit without pressuring everyone.

professor.name: Dr. Reyes
professor.text: We've been examining how audiences judge the credibility of what they see online. As tools for generating realistic images become widespread, some news organizations have started attaching a visible label whenever a picture was created or substantially altered by artificial intelligence. Supporters say such labels protect readers from deception, while critics worry that constant warnings will make people distrust legitimate photography as well. Should news outlets be required to label AI-generated images? Explain your reasoning.

students.name: Karen
students.text: Required labeling seems essential to me. When readers cannot distinguish a real photograph from a fabricated one, misinformation spreads faster and public trust erodes. A clear, standardized label costs the outlet almost nothing, and it lets each person decide consciously how much weight to give a particular image.

students.name: Marco
students.text: I understand the appeal, but I doubt that blanket labels solve much. Determined manipulators will simply publish through unregulated channels, while honest newsrooms get burdened with disclaimers on ordinary edited photos. I would rather invest in teaching media literacy so audiences learn to question suspicious content themselves.
